front: What laptop RAM form factor is used instead of desktop DIMM, and how many pins does DDR5 use?
back: SODIMM (Small Outline DIMM). DDR4 SODIMM = 260 pins, DDR5 SODIMM = 262 pins.

front: Why can't you always upgrade RAM on an ultrabook/thin laptop?
back: Some thin designs solder RAM directly to the motherboard, making it non-upgradeable.

front: What storage form factor replaced 2.5" SATA drives as the performance standard in laptops?
back: M.2 NVMe SSDs, connected via PCIe lanes instead of the SATA bus — much faster than SATA SSDs.

front: What is laptop battery calibration and what does it actually fix?
back: Fully charge, fully discharge, then fully charge again. It resets the battery meter's reported percentage accuracy — it does not restore lost capacity.

front: Li-ion vs Li-poly laptop batteries — what's the practical difference?
back: Li-ion is the standard chemistry. Li-poly is used in some ultra-thin laptops because it can be shaped/molded flexibly.

front: What's the difference between a docking station and a port replicator?
back: A docking station usually adds real functionality (extra GPU output, more ports than the laptop has). A port replicator just extends the laptop's existing ports.

front: Where do laptop wifi/Bluetooth antenna wires typically route, and why?
back: From the wireless card (mini PCIe or M.2, usually in the base) through the hinge to antenna leads embedded in the screen bezel.

front: What is eSIM and how does it differ from a physical SIM card?
back: An embedded, programmable digital SIM built into the device — no physical card swap needed to change carriers/plans.

front: What does airplane mode disable, and what can typically be re-enabled individually afterward?
back: Disables all radios at once (cellular, wifi, Bluetooth). Wifi and Bluetooth can usually be re-enabled individually while airplane mode stays on.

front: What is MDM (Mobile Device Management) used for?
back: A corporate tool to enforce policy on mobile devices — remote wipe, push apps, enforce passcodes/encryption on managed devices.

front: BYOD vs COPE vs COBO — what's the difference?
back: BYOD = bring your own device (personal device, managed via MDM profile). COPE = corporate owned, personally enabled. COBO = corporate owned, business only.

front: What does containerization do on a mobile device?
back: Separates work apps/data from personal data on the same physical device.

front: A phone shows severe battery drain — name three common causes.
back: Degraded battery health, excessive background app refresh/activity, or screen brightness too high (a failing battery is also a common cause).

front: What's the general difference between 4G LTE and 5G cellular?
back: 5G offers much higher speed and lower latency, using higher-frequency bands (including short-range mmWave) plus sub-6GHz bands for wider coverage.
